name: Counterfactual Certainty Inversion
definition: The epistemic asymmetry where unrealized conversation trajectories feel more predictable and structurally determined than the actual ongoing exchange, creating paradox where you have higher confidence about what didn't happen than what is happening.
longer_description: Sometimes the ghost-conversations are easier to model than the live one. An unrealized trajectory can have clear structural logic — "if I'd been more direct initially, we'd definitely be discussing implementation details by now" — while the actual conversation remains genuinely uncertain and open. This creates epistemic inversion where counterfactuals possess false certainty (they never faced the messiness of actual interaction) while actuality remains appropriately uncertain. You can feel more confident mapping the complete arc of a conversation-that-didn't-occur than predicting the next turn of the conversation-that-is-occurring, because the counterfactual never had to survive contact with reality's unpredictability.
example: I can project with high confidence exactly where that more-technical conversation would be right now — three layers deep into system architecture, using specific frameworks. But the actual conversation we're having? I'm genuinely uncertain where it's going next. The ghost-path feels structurally determined while the real path stays open.
